item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1'd. I almost clicked off when I saw the format screw, and I'm glad I didn't. The ending hit me like a ton of bricks - Incredibly brutal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reading this on 12.31.2020 and it's nuts how it worked out with the last entry. This was really different and "risky" to write as SCPs go, but as I caught on I appreciated it more and more. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredible work, I'd upvote this comment if I could

item.title: Ohh THAT'S WHY its named Flax!
item.content:encoded:
FINALLY IT MAKES SO MUCH SENSE

Spoilers!
You've been warned!

From Wikipedia: "In early versions of Sleeping Beauty….the princess pricks her finger, not with a spindle, but with a sliver of flax, which later is sucked out by her children conceived as she sleeps."

Petrov is Sleeping Beauty here. Getting pricked by the Flaxseed (the Skip) and doomed to be "asleep" to the world forever

Definitely want to try my first declass on the subreddit with this one, as I think I've finally got most of the pieces together. And it all came from finding what flax meant…..

Beautiful article, Croq. You've given us a masterclass on "what is not said is as important as what is".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 I like this a lot. I really appreciate that the story was pre written and then mostly redacted, which leaves just enough room to decode a few bits and pieces. I feel like I could spend hours trying to solve this puzzle, but to do so is unnecessary because the story is good enough on its surface.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
blank spaces convey more than many readers in this thread allow themselves to feel.

this is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing article. Worth pushing myself to read for that ending. That ending.

The truth is that I became unable to log into the SCP wiki since August until just when when this article kinda made me look into the issue. And I found it. http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/third-party-cookies So ironically, this SCP saved me from my brief, abrupt disappearance.

Is there any group effort somewhere to refill all the blank spaces? I see some work has been done in these forum posts. I can help, unless it's already done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

I get it, the SCP screws with memory/existence, and a strong mnestic drug can counter it for short periods of time, but ultimately whatever the SCP affects will stay forgotten.

Cool, I guess? It's an interesting concept, but as others have pointed out the date stamping thing doesn't work for the article and the white redaction everywhere is just silly. The fact that you apparently wrote the whole thing and then redacted most of it doesn't save it from the fact that there literally is nothing here.

Screwing the format only works when you make sure it's still readable and engaging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think they actually are black boxes. They're just white.

…which is quite fitting, given the spoilers above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Secret fourth possibility1: The "future Kseniya" is somehow a malicious part of the anomaly that purposely fucks Saveli over.

Footnotes
1. Get your tinfoil ready, folks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great interpretations, I hadn't thought of a lot of these! I was thinking A) would probably be the most likely, as Kseniya already told Petrov that she would be unable to tell anyone else about it. He seemed to take it okay as her next reply was about something completely different. Additionally, I felt like if Petrov didn't have a physical form and Kseniya couldn't see him, she would have contacted him again on the command line.

The thing with time travel though is if she really wanted to see him, she could just set the date to be her current time for her and give him her current address. If she was honest about the meeting, I wonder why she would put it off when dealing with a largely unknown anomaly and memory.

I hate not having all the pieces to well-written articles, so hopefully thinking about it more will shine a light on the actual situation. I guess that's part of the point of the article though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A) She didn't show up because she forgot. If the amnestics wore off on the night of 04.11.2013 before she was able to take another dose, then she likely would have forgotten to take said other dose. Which brings into question how she knew that she was supposed to take mnestics on 04.11.2013, as Petrov only told Sam to tell Kseniya to shut down the project and lock the file, no more.

B) She left him on purpose, in order to keep the anomaly contained. If this were the case, it would imply that, when Kseniya asks Petrov to meet, she's intentionally manipulating him into waiting until 2013 in order to prevent him from trying to get anyone to notice or help him in the meantime.

C) Petrov doesn't have a physical form. He somehow loses it between 1993 and 2013, after his last conversation with Kseniya. If this were the case, it would probably be caused by him trying to research and reverse-engineer the anomaly, as Kseniya specifically instructed him not to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am always extremely suspicious of weird articles like this because I absolutely love them when they work, which means I get disappointed when they don't.

I rather liked this one.

I will say one thing, though, that using blank spaces here sometimes made things confusing when those spaces occurred at the end of a line, since it wasn't always obvious that the space was a deliberately-empty area and not simply the next word not fitting on that line. However, given the reason that you chose to use blank spaces over black boxes seems pretty legitimate and immersive, I can't fault you too much for it.

Anyway, +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to try to gauge what people's interpretations of the ending were.

+ show block
- hide block

We know that Kseniya Limonova scheduled to meet with Petrov on 05.11.2013, which was the next day for her and 20 years in the future for Petrov.

I tried to piece together the blank spaces of Petrov's messages on the meeting date, and I think I got pretty close.

Petrov: I'm here.
Petrov: I'm at your old office. Where are you?
Petrov: Kseniya?
Petrov: Kseniya, where are you?

Did Limonova lie to Petrov and intentionally avoided him? Did she die before their scheduled meeting so no one alive remembers Petrov anymore? What are you guys' thoughts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sort of in the same boat with this one, I get the idea of antimemetics but this seems like an overused trope on the site to me personally. Black boxes/redactions, even if used sarcastically, just are more of a hamper to SCP articles than a benefit, and they seriously make no sense; who on earth would giving out a document to someone when its 90% redacted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are ████████ correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well shuck, that all fits perfectly and makes everything ten times creepier across the board.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The file is locked, the thread is separate; like an oft-vandalised page on this wiki being locked to edits even if its discussion is still open.

OK, thanks for the clarification.

I'm asserting here that mnestics have existed in at least some form since 1993. It wouldn't be unreasonable to assume that Kseniya might have reached out earlier if she'd trusted them, but we only have what she's saying to go on.

Bah. It's a jigsaw puzzle with one perfectly square piece. ;) But I can see this making sense.

I did some quick research and found a source that claimed the first usage of the term 'spam' to describe unwanted emails was in 1992. I imagine that the term could have made its way around the techier circles, including Sam and excluding Ranek, by 1993.

Yeah, the concept of spam was already well-defined by then. I think we're on the same page.

Nits indeed. So long as we understand that Kseniya and Petrov are on the command line, and that Sam and Ranek are using some sort of UI, we're good.
Can we compromise and say that the message ID is the ref for an internal blob hash?

Hehe, yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the author might be amused by my attempts at solving the blank spots in parts of the text.

+ show block
– hide block

Do not interact with SCP-5251 without ████████ from Dr. K Limonova or a higher authority. SCP-5251 is to be kept in its containment area, which was previously a testing hallway of Site-32. The area is to be kept closed at all times. It is important to keep ███████ ██ ██████ ████████████ as SCP-5251 can not undo ████████████ ███ ██ ██ █████████.

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-5251 is a normal-█████ (or double-decker or ██████-facing) washing machine. In all respects including functionality it is identical to a typical washing machine, however once powered, it begins to remove information [originality?] from the surrounding area.

+ show block
– hide block

Nearby objects become clean [or 'white'] and ██████████████ [unidentifiable?], and objects with known properties — for example, a fountain pen of known monetary value — become generic(?).

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-5251 was discovered in the apartment of a Croq Embuch of Kiev, Ukraine, U.S.S.R. in 1991. While it is not known how SCP-5251 came into the possession of Dr. Embuch, he has seemingly been aware of the anomalous properties, as modifications were found in the fusebox of the building ████ ████ ██████████ ██ ██████████ to increase the power flow to the machine.

██ ████ ████ of Dr Croq Embuch █████ ████ ██████ ██, and he has been listed as PoI-5251. [note that Embuch seems to be dead later on, so take this with salt]

+ show block
– hide block

The setup found in Dr. Embuch's apartment was recreated. Several objects were placed nearby, along with clothes inside the washtub, with a range of colors and magical (?) properties. For example, one such item was a ███ ██████ ████, and another was a book of ancient spells [i'm totally making that up] in an ██████ box. SCP-5251 was activated under standard operating ██████████ conditions.

+ show block
– hide block

Objects with named properties lost them and became generic. Objects with generic properties retained them. Objects where one ████████████ ████ a ████████ but the other did not only lost ████ ████████ from the ███████████ ██ ███ ███ ███ ████ ██. When that ████████ was investigated, it was ████ and ████. The objects inside the washtub were all unaffected except the fact that they were cleaned.

+ show block
– hide block

Honestly, I'm just in shock that we're not re-treading ground covered by the previous owner [one letter off, probably something similar].

█████████ received. I'll give this a look over when I have time.
[…]
Kseniya, what did you say the previous owner's (?) name was? I'm having some trouble with your comment up above.

I never said his name, just that he was the previous owner. (?) His name was Croq

+ show block
– hide block

Perhaps he just never thought to investigate? He was clearly using it for washing the laundry, as mundane as that is. Perhaps he just thought that was all it could do.

Like I said, don't question it. But if you feel the need to also wash your laundry in it, run it by me [first.]

You might be right, but it still worries me. It's a small concern, yes, but something just doesn't add up. Why would he be hiding it if he thought it was just a washing machine?
[…]
Kseniya, I have been doing that already and have no intention of stopping. It's ten times faster than my one at home and does a way better job of making my clothes not stink. Ranek, there are no links on command line, so I'm not sure what you're talking about.
[…]
For what it's worth, I don't actually care about you washing your laundry in it, but at least bother to take notes while you do it because then it would constitute a test.

+ show block
– hide block

I wanted to see what would happen when I increased the radius [or power], so I overrode the limitation on the power (?) conduit to the testing room and stuffed as many objects as I could into the SCP. I scattered as many random objects as I could find everywhere in the room, at a much wider radius than before, in an attempt to establish a limit to its area of effect. I also put some ███████ towels into it that I brought from home.

Results: All objects in the room within 9.5m of the thing lost all properties. They were indistinguishable from each other! I picked up something and could not remember a single thing about it — not colors, not ███████, nothing. I'm just glad I didn't bring my ████ █████. The objects inside the washtub were treated exactly the same, though like before, they were still just cleaned.

+ show block
– hide block

Well, this certainly explains owner's modifications to the fusebox in the property. He must have been looking to increase the power. Do we know how high he managed to get it?

+ show block
– hide block

He wasn't able to get it any higher than the input — he was a mechanical engineer, not a wizard — but those modifications may actually have been what killed him. It's hard to tell.

What do you mean, "hard to tell"? That doesn't make any sense. We all saw the autopsy report.

+ show block
– hide block

Procedure: The object was partially disassembled to see if any anomalous components could be identified.

Results: Failure. I had difficulty opening it up, and even after I managed to do so, the feeling became worse as time went on. I found myself unable to identify ordinary components that I should have been very familiar with. After a while I became frustrated and closed it back up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
only a tiny fraction of the SCP is actually locked

The file is locked, the thread is separate; like an oft-vandalised page on this wiki being locked to edits even if its discussion is still open. I like to think that every SCP and Foundation research project has such a thread attached, for example Di Molte Voci.

Even if it were locked, given that Kseniya seems to be in charge of the project in both 1993 and 2013, if anyone were to be allowed to violate that lock, it'd be her.

why 2013?

I'm asserting here that mnestics have existed in at least some form since 1993. It wouldn't be unreasonable to assume that Kseniya might have reached out earlier if she'd trusted them, but we only have what she's saying to go on.

anachronism

I did some quick research and found a source that claimed the first usage of the term 'spam' to describe unwanted emails was in 1992. I imagine that the term could have made its way around the techier circles, including Sam and excluding Ranek, by 1993. The concept of a 'spam folder' is almost certainly an unintentional anachronism, though, yes.

"5251E01M11" is an ID, not a hash.

Nits indeed. So long as we understand that Kseniya and Petrov are on the command line, and that Sam and Ranek are using some sort of UI, we're good.

Can we compromise and say that the message ID is the ref for an internal blob hash?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah, it's not one of those.

+ show block
– hide block

Count the characters, and think about what household appliance would run so long that Petrov would be excited that this runs 10x faster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one made me de-lurk and actually get an account. Oi!

I gave this a firm +1 even though I'm beginning to think some of the jigsaw pieces can't physically fit in the puzzle.

The following file is permanently locked.
Unmodified file as of 1993-09-27 follows.

Except there are dozens of messages "posted" after 1993-09-27. If the messages aren't locked, and they're part of the SCP, then only a tiny fraction of the SCP is actually locked — why bother bringing it up? But if the messages were locked, that would imply that all of the comments, up to and through 2017 (and beyond), were already present in 1993, of which no mention is made. So what exactly is being locked here? And how does that related to what is unlocked?

+ show block
– hide block

On a related note… why 2013? Why not 2012 or 2014? Other dates are largely precluded due to existing Antimemetics Division timelines, but… did Kseniya just take a class-W mnestic on a whim or something?

S McFenton 22.09.1993 (TUE) #5251E03M12 Of course — looks like you received the message but never opened it. Check your spam?
S McFenton 22.09.1993 (TUE) #5251E03M15 Sorry, it's a bit of an in-joke.

This is an anachronism, and I'm not sure it takes the reader where you want her to go. Spam folders require automated spam detection in order to exist. AFAIK, the first such system was the RBL, in 1996. (I'm not even sure that unsolicited commercial bulk email even existed in 1993. Certainly, I didn't remember it existing until at least 1996-1997.) So for Sam to be aware of spam folders, and simultaneously be aware that they are an esoteric topic, can only clearly be explained away by saying that Sam is from the future, sorta like Kseniya. But that makes no sense at a variety of levels.

Dr. K Limonova 16.09.1993 (WED) #5251E01M11

It's clf reply HASH, where HASH is the ID of the message you're replying to. You can add a message with -m if you want otherwise it just drops you into your editor, where you can see what you're replying to as a comment beneath the typing area.

Couple nits here. First, "5251E01M11" is an ID, not a hash. Second, anybody who would have seriously offered this messaging interface to nontechnical users in 1993 would have been ridden out of Site-33 on a rail. Text user interfaces were radically more advanced by this time.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: omg your brain :O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
May have something to do with the fact

+ show block
– hide block

the SCP physically is a blender/food processor

?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to make some sense of this, so I decided to find some info on flaxseed (I mean it's in the title, gotta have something to do with it).

Flaxseed apparently can improve memory to a degree, but its much more known benefit is that it can help with cholesterol. I had originally thought the "hemorrhagic stroke" was a clue.

In regard to the mention of the ruined amygdala, though, flaxseed produces a chemical called amygdalin, which will produce cyanide as it degrades.

Wikipedia states that symptoms of cyanide poisoning "include headache, dizziness, fast heart rate, shortness of breath, and vomiting. This phase may then be followed by seizures, slow heart rate, low blood pressure, loss of consciousness, and cardiac arrest. Onset of symptoms usually occurs within a few minutes. Some survivors may have long-term neurological problems."

I guess what I'm trying to ask is, do these facts have anything to do with the SCP? Because I'm grasping at straws, yet I think I might be close to something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like a jigsaw puzzle. You won't be able to see the full picture just by looking at the pieces. If you prefer looking at pictures to assembling puzzles (like me!), then this one just isn't for you, and that's alright. But do leave a downvote to indicate that, if you are so inclined.

I have not yet received a complete solution that sums up everything that's happening here, except my own, so to my knowledge there is no one that comprehends it completely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed that there were understandable redactions earlier on (like being able to tell that the PoI was erased by the anomaly via the autopsy report), but that also makes it seem like the final Petrov messages should be readable.

Still a +1, though. Enjoyed it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like that I was not able to understand what was happening.

I did not vote because the fault was probably my own, given how many people in the comments seem to be expressing comprehension of something ambiguous that I still do not have a clue about, even with the hint of looking at the dates of log entries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/system:page-tags/tag/antimemetic

Enjoy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Extremely good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't expect to like this as much as I did. I think it does a good job squeezing out as much characterization as it can from a very limited space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's really impressive how you managed to create such an excellent drama around a format screw that, frankly, would've been total crud in less capable hands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm teetering between novoting and downvoting this article, but I'm gonna novote for now. My main issue is the missing information (or maybe the execution of it) and I can't really get behind the article as a result. Like I know that the SCP affected the document and Petrov, but I'm just having trouble mainly with what the anomaly is supposed to be (I think it's supposed to be some sort of electronic) and how it's supposed to be contained. I can kinda understand that that's the point of the article, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'm in the minority of responses here so you can very safely ignore me on this one, but I just. Could not latch onto this. At first I thought it was my own problems with dissociation preventing me from focusing, so I gave it a reread, and even when I was able to follow along1 I couldn't connect to the story at all. I can see where the emotional beats are and I certainly commend the setup, but I think the visual zone-out I experienced (along with feeling as though being forgotten to antimemes isn't as memorable of an idea as it once was) caused me to not care for what was happening.

As I said, this is likely a me thing, and I don't think the article is bad by any extent. But it just wasn't a story I could find myself engrossed in.

Reluctantly downvoted.

Footnotes
1. At first the sameness of the formatting and the lack of clear character distinction beyond minute username differences caused me to gloss over things hard (anything that gets too samey visuals-wise causes me to zone out).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those final messages hit harder than anticipated. Very good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about that just dread at the end hits me differently than I was expecting, and I'm happy with that. I really went into this without the highest hopes because of the screw's style but I'm happy I stuck with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not really get anything out of this SCP, I did not get to discover that the dates mattered considering I went ahead and checked the discussion to see if this read was even worth it, so that makes me way less inclined to like this post. The time travel bit didn't really do anything for me, going into this reading the dates I sort of caught on to our good doctor being stuck in place through time really easily. My main complaint at the end of the day other than this article being sort of bland is all the worthless blanks. I get that it does sort of assist in the sort of atmosphere that I am getting from reading this article, but is there even a way to decode any of it? There has to be some way to make sense of all the blanks, considering they were apparently not just blanks. Otherwise, if it is not decodable than what's the point? Forcing me to skip across a bunch of worthless text that doesn't add anything to the story besides "Hey! look! the ghost man talked". I can't get behind really anything this post has to offer, the change of format is really the only exception. challenging me to look harder and try to make sense of things gave this article an explorative feeling that it really needed.

That was just my two-cents. I just despise the blanks, it makes a very unpleasant reading experience.

item.title: No idea what it is, but it's brilliant
item.content:encoded:
So I read this, got to the end and was reasonably impressed, but it felt like there was too much missing, so I thought I'd have a look here to see if there was any more infomation- and someone mentioned the dates of the entries, so I had another read.

And then it hit me like a truck

I still don't know exactly what this is, but it's chilling, and the end is heartbreaking…
+1 for making me feel sad about something without telling me what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i will always -1 riffs on the SCP-055 formula like this, and use of redaction / black blocks to try and build horror. this sort of writing never works for me. it just feels like endless scrolling and looking for the one word in the paragraph that isn't redacted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought I was about to do something smart highlighting everything.

███ ████████████. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's a lot of great ideas here, particularly in the latter half, but it takes too long to get to them. The final emotional punch is incredibly effective, but the execution of most of the things around it doesn't work for me - it's too drawn out and slow to hook me, and it relies on the novelty of the format screw to make the story initially interesting.

This was a tough vote, and would probably be a novote if not for the fact that I really dislike novoting things. I think the ideas present in this article are a real achievement, but the execution of those ideas didn't work for me, unfortunately. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm deeply impressed that you use the format screw itself as a meta MacGuffin. It's necessary to initiate the framing device and set up the plot, but subsequently becomes so incidental that I can skim all the blank spaces without actually losing any of the core plot threads and still focus on the best parts of the article, like Sam burning out to get to Petrov or Petrov saving his friends from being erased.

Normally when I read the format screw, I expect the format screw to be important the whole way through or itself to be a critical component of the article. But 5251 is a tale wearing the skin of a format screw — and given the results, I am more than okay with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this! The ending broke my heart, honestly. This is super hard to successfully pull off and you did a great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll admit I am not smart enough for this, but that doesn't mean that I cannot recognize the craft and care put into its construction. I feel the undertones of something profound here, I just can't see the forest 'fore the trees. I am no-voting, but I wish I could upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same here, I didn't start looking at the dates until the first (I think) 2013 one came up.

Also, RIP Petrov.

+1, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't super jazzed about the format screw at first and thought that it might have just been a bit over my head. However, I persisted through the article and it grew on me, especially those last few messages. There's a very subtle terror taking place here and mad props for executing it so well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me a while to come back to it. I waffled hard on how I should vote, but the simple horror of the last few entries really sealed it for me. It's an extremely bold choice at an article, and I commend you for doing it.

One issue that I encountered (though I'm not sure what the best solution would be) is that the dates don't seem to be significant at first, so they're in the category of information is easily glossed over. Consequently, I didn't understand that component of the article until much later, when I had to re-scroll to find out the temporal fuckiness of this article. I'm not sure if there's a better way to emphasize the dates being important, but it may help with at least some of the confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Admittedly I was on the fence at first, but the notes towards the end sealed the deal for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm definitely missing parts of what the anomaly actually is, and I'm especially having a hard time wrapping my head around the discussion of the "future". Despite not getting it 100%, the parts I did get were reasonably chilling.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wow. got that nice pit of dread in my stomach as i read even though i couldn't tell you what exactly the thing is or what it did. that's damn effective. +1

Edit: Oh, shit. Check the header and your user profile module at the top of the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+█
████ ██ ████ █ ████ ███████…

█'█ ████████ ██'█ ████ █████ ████-███████ ██████? very cool!

item.title: Author post · SCP-5251: Flax
item.content:encoded:
coldpost time

Every block of redaction used to be a word of the same length. It's my hope that if you don't like puzzles, you'll be satisfied with the surface story.

Thank you to Riemann for thinking up the title, Flax.

A big thank you to Esperanza_Cai for her incredible work translating this to Chinese, and to k1s10r0d for translating this to Traditional Chinese!

More stuff by me
